does: This paper introduces Minerva-Ego, a benchmark dataset for evaluating complex reasoning in egocentric videos. The dataset extends existing egocentric video sources with challenging multi-step multimodal questions requiring both visual and temporal understanding. Crucially, it includes spatiotemporally-dense human annotations of reasoning traces and object-of-interest masks that specify where and when relevant information appears in videos. Benchmarking experiments reveal substantial performance gaps between state-of-the-art models and human performance. The authors demonstrate that prompting frontier vision-language models with spatiotemporal hints—explicit information about relevant objects' locations and temporal positions—yields significant performance improvements, suggesting that directing model attention to critical spatiotemporal regions is key to improving reasoning.
connects: This work builds on recent progress in video understanding and egocentric AI, which has expanded from simple action recognition toward complex reasoning tasks. It extends prior multimodal benchmarks like those in embodied AI and egocentric vision by introducing dense intermediate supervision through reasoning traces and spatiotemporal annotations. The paper responds to limitations in existing video QA benchmarks that only evaluate final outputs without visibility into reasoning steps. It connects to growing research on prompt engineering and in-context learning for vision-language models, which has shown that structured hints can guide model reasoning. The focus on egocentric perspective relates to embodied AI research where agents must understand scenes from first-person viewpoints with temporal dynamics.
enables: This benchmark enables future work on interpretable video reasoning systems that can explain their predictions through spatiotemporal attention. By providing dense annotations of reasoning steps, it supports training approaches that supervise intermediate reasoning rather than only final answers. The finding that spatial and temporal hints substantially improve performance opens directions for research on adaptive prompting strategies and attention mechanisms in multimodal models. The dataset could facilitate development of methods that more robustly ground reasoning in visual evidence, reducing hallucinations in embodied AI systems. Future work could explore whether models can learn to automatically identify critical spatiotemporal regions without explicit hints, advancing toward more self-directed visual reasoning in egocentric scenarios.
